refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' (a boundary condition) and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated metric rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct end-to-end packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure across Telemetry (sensor→operator) and Tasking (operator→vehicle) paths. Log packet loss statistics over extended operational sessions (≥8 hours) and validate against acceptance criterion.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit operator commands (Tasking) from tele-operator console to vehicle with packet loss ≤ customer threshold.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit vehicle sensor telemetry (Telemetry) from vehicle to tele-operator console with packet loss ≤ customer threshold.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication quality degradation (packet loss exceeding threshold) via SafetyAlert to Safety Services.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall implement adaptive or redundant communication strategies to maintain packet loss within threshold under variable network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement end-to-end packet loss monitoring with real-time threshold detection and automatic failover to redundant network path (dual-link architecture with seamless switchover).
DP-B: Implement adaptive codec/compression and selective packet prioritization (QoS-based filtering) to reduce effective packet loss impact on critical commands and telemetry.
DP-C: Implement forward error correction (FEC) and packet retransmission protocol with bounded latency to recover lost packets within operator reaction time window.
DP-D: Implement network path diversity (multi-carrier, multi-protocol) with intelligent routing to avoid congestion and minimize packet loss probability.

DP-E: Implement hybrid approach combining real-time packet loss monitoring (DP-A), adaptive prioritization (DP-B), and FEC/retransmission (DP-C) with safety-critical fallback to manual operation or SafetyAlert escalation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-E: Implement a multi-layered teleoperation communication architecture combining: (1) real-time packet loss monitoring with threshold detection and SafetyAlert escalation, (2) adaptive QoS-based packet prioritization (critical commands/telemetry prioritized over non-critical), (3) forward error correction and bounded-latency retransmission for critical packets, and (4) graceful degradation with operator notification and safety-critical fallback triggers when packet loss exceeds threshold.

potential_sources: DP-E couples teleoperation communication quality to Safety Services (SafetyAlert interface) and Vehicle command execution (Tasking interface). This is acceptable and intentional: safety-critical packet loss detection must trigger SafetyAlert.
DP-E couples to Network Link quality assumptions. Mitigation: DP-E is designed to work within 'agreed network conditions' boundary; customer must specify acceptable network SLA.
DP-E couples operator training/procedure to adaptive behavior. Mitigation: operator must understand QoS prioritization and fallback triggers; training and worksite safety plan required.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is minimal and justified. DP-E is a communication architecture decision that logically decouples from vehicle control logic (Vehicle DP) and safety decision logic (Safety Services DP). The interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined boundaries.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer will define acceptable network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth, carrier type) and packet loss threshold before detailed design.
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle and Teleoperation modules can implement QoS tagging and prioritization at packet level.
ASSUMPTION: Operator reaction time window is ≥200ms (typical for heavy equipment); FEC/retransmission latency must stay within this bound.
RISK: If network conditions are worse than 'agreed' baseline, packet loss may exceed threshold despite DP-E implementation. Mitigation: SafetyAlert escalation and fallback to manual operation.
RISK: FEC overhead and retransmission latency may degrade real-time responsiveness if not carefully tuned. Mitigation: bounded-latency design and operator training.

RISK: Adaptive prioritization may deprioritize non-critical telemetry (e.g., equipment diagnostics), reducing operator situational awareness. Mitigation: clear operator interface showing which telemetry is degraded.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-E is sound but should be refined to explicitly specify: (1) packet loss detection threshold and escalation logic (e.g., 0.5% triggers warning, 1% triggers SafetyAlert), (2) FEC scheme and retransmission timeout (must be ≤operator reaction time), (3) QoS priority levels and which packets fall into each level, (4) fallback behavior (e.g., switch to manual operation, reduce speed, alert operator). These details will be decomposed in next level.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication architecture for remote equipment operation, including packet loss monitoring, adaptive QoS, FEC/retransmission, and SafetyAlert escalation. Teleoperation interfaces with Vehicle (via Tasking and Telemetry), Safety Services (via SafetyAlert), and Network Link (via Network Link interface). Teleoperation does NOT own the physical network infrastructure (Network Link) or vehicle control logic (Vehicle) or safety decision logic (Safety Services).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator commands (steering, speed, bucket control, etc.) transmitted from Teleoperation to Vehicle with QoS prioritization and retransmission. Tasking packets are marked as critical and receive highest priority in DP-E.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle sensor data (position, orientation, load, diagnostics, etc.) transmitted from Vehicle to Teleoperation. Telemetry packets are monitored for loss; critical telemetry (position, orientation) prioritized over non-critical (diagnostics). Packet loss on Telemetry is measured and reported.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Alerts transmitted from Teleoperation to Safety Services when packet loss exceeds threshold or communication quality degrades. SafetyAlert triggers safety-critical fallback (e.g., operator notification, speed reduction, manual operation mode).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network transport (carrier, protocol, bandwidth) used by Teleoperation to send Tasking and receive Telemetry. Teleoperation monitors Network Link quality (latency, jitter, loss) and adapts QoS accordingly. Network Link is owned by infrastructure, not Teleoperation.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Packet loss monitoring and communication quality are system-level concerns, but the detailed implementation (FEC, QoS, retransmission) should be localized to Teleoperation module, not spread across NGHE Solution. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility and make testing/ownership unclear. Containment score is low because NGHE Solution should delegate to Teleoperation.

Vehicle module owns vehicle control logic and sensor acquisition. Packet loss monitoring and communication quality are NOT vehicle concerns; they are teleoperation concerns. Assigning to Vehicle would couple vehicle control to communication architecture, violating separation of concerns. Vehicle should only implement Tasking reception and Telemetry transmission; Teleoperation owns quality assurance.

Teleoperation module is the natural owner of teleoperation communication quality. DP-E (packet loss monitoring, adaptive QoS, FEC, SafetyAlert escalation) is entirely within Teleoperation's scope. Teleoperation interfaces with Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry), Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and Network Link (transport). Containment is very high; diffusion risk is minimal. This is the correct assignment.

Safety Services owns safety decision logic and SafetyAlert generation. Packet loss monitoring is a communication concern, not a safety decision concern. However, Safety Services must receive SafetyAlert from Teleoperation and decide on fallback actions (e.g., operator notification, speed reduction). Assigning packet loss monitoring to Safety Services would couple safety logic to communication architecture. Containment score is moderate because Safety Services receives SafetyAlert output but does not own the monitoring logic.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of CR-NGHE-009 implementation. Teleoperation naturally contains the communication architecture (packet loss monitoring, adaptive QoS, FEC, retransmission) and interfaces with Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry), Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and Network Link (transport). Assigning to Teleoperation maintains clear separation of concerns: Teleoperation owns communication quality, Vehicle owns control logic, Safety Services owns safety decisions. Containment score of 0.95 reflects high cohesion and low diffusion.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may become large if it also owns operator console UI, command encoding, telemetry decoding, and network protocol stack. Mitigation: decompose Teleoperation into sub-modules (e.g., Communication Controller, QoS Manager, FEC Engine, Operator Interface) in next level.

RISK: Teleoperation depends on Vehicle and Safety Services modules being available and responsive. If Vehicle does not implement Tasking reception correctly, or Safety Services does not respond to SafetyAlert, DP-E will fail. Mitigation: define clear interface contracts and integration tests.

RISK: Teleoperation depends on Network Link quality. If network conditions are worse than 'agreed' baseline, packet loss may exceed threshold despite DP-E. Mitigation: SafetyAlert escalation and fallback to manual operation; customer must validate network SLA.

RISK: Teleoperation module may need to be distributed across operator console (tele-operator side) and vehicle (vehicle-side) for FEC/retransmission to work. Mitigation: clarify in next level whether Teleoperation is a single logical module with distributed implementation, or split into Teleoperation-Console and Teleoperation-Vehicle sub-modules.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Teleoperation assignment is sound. Recommended refinements: (1) Clarify whether Teleoperation is a single logical module with distributed implementation (console + vehicle) or split into sub-modules. (2) Define sub-module boundaries for Communication Controller, QoS Manager, FEC Engine, and Operator Interface to avoid diffusion. (3) Specify interface contracts for Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link (e.g., packet format, latency bounds, error handling). (4) Identify dependencies on Vehicle and Safety Services modules and define integration test strategy.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Refined FR (packet loss ≤ customer threshold under agreed network conditions) is directly addressed by selected DP-E (multi-layered communication architecture with monitoring, adaptive QoS, FEC, and SafetyAlert escalation). DP-E is a complete and logically sound solution to the FR. No decoupling or missing logic detected. FR is clear, measurable, and verifiable; DP is implementable and testable.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK: DP-E includes multiple sub-concerns (packet loss monitoring, adaptive QoS, FEC, retransmission, SafetyAlert escalation). If Teleoperation module is not further decomposed, these concerns may diffuse across multiple code files or sub-systems. Mitigation: decompose Teleoperation into sub-modules (Communication Controller, QoS Manager, FEC Engine) in next level to maintain cohesion.

DIFFUSION RISK: DP-E depends on Vehicle and Safety Services modules implementing their parts correctly (Tasking reception, Telemetry transmission, SafetyAlert response). If these modules are not well-designed, Teleoperation may need to compensate, leading to diffusion. Mitigation: define clear interface contracts and integration tests.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.95) for DP-E. All core logic (packet loss monitoring, adaptive QoS, FEC, retransmission, SafetyAlert escalation) is naturally contained within Teleoperation. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal. No diffusion across other modules detected at this level. Containment hypothesis is validated.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-009 is feasible. Packet loss can be measured end-to-end using packet capture and analysis tools. Acceptance criterion (≤1% under agreed network conditions) is measurable and testable. Verification strategy: (1) conduct lab testing with simulated network conditions, (2) conduct field testing under real network conditions agreed with customer, (3) log packet loss statistics over extended operational sessions, (4) validate SafetyAlert escalation when threshold is exceeded. Verification is straightforward and does not require specialized equipment beyond standard network monitoring tools.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of CR-NGHE-009 to Teleoperation module is clear. Teleoperation owns the end-to-end communication architecture and is responsible for maintaining packet loss within threshold. Interfaces with Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry), Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and Network Link (transport) are clearly defined. Ownership and responsibility are unambiguous. No allocation conflicts detected.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1: Decompose Teleoperation module in next level into sub-modules (Communication Controller, QoS Manager, FEC Engine, Operator Interface) to avoid diffusion and maintain cohesion.
REVISION 2: Define detailed interface contracts for Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link (packet format, latency bounds, error handling, QoS tags) to enable integration testing.

REVISION 3: Clarify whether Teleoperation is a single logical module with distributed implementation (console + vehicle) or split into Teleoperation-Console and Teleoperation-Vehicle sub-modules. This affects ownership and testing strategy.

REVISION 4: Define packet loss detection threshold and escalation logic (e.g., 0.5% triggers warning, 1% triggers SafetyAlert) and fallback behavior (e.g., switch to manual operation, reduce speed, alert operator) in next level.

REVISION 5: Identify and document dependencies on Vehicle and Safety Services modules. Define integration test strategy to validate that Vehicle implements Tasking reception correctly and Safety Services responds to SafetyAlert appropriately.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-009 is a complex, multi-faceted requirement that spans communication architecture, safety escalation, and operator interaction. The refined FR is clear and measurable, the selected DP-E is logically sound and addresses all aspects of the requirement, and the assigned IM (Teleoperation) is the correct owner with high containment (0.95). However, DP-E includes multiple sub-concerns (packet loss monitoring, adaptive QoS, FEC, retransmission, SafetyAlert escalation) that should be decomposed into sub-FRs and sub-DPs in the next level to avoid diffusion and maintain architectural clarity. The four sub-FRs identified in the refined_fr section (FR-NGHE-009.1 through FR-NGHE-009.4) provide a clear decomposition path. Proceeding to the next level will enable detailed design of each sub-concern and allocation to sub-modules within Teleoperation.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit operator commands (Tasking) from tele-operator console to vehicle with packet loss ≤ customer threshold.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit vehicle sensor telemetry (Telemetry) from vehicle to tele-operator console with packet loss ≤ customer threshold.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication quality degradation (packet loss exceeding threshold) via SafetyAlert to Safety Services.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall implement adaptive or redundant communication strategies to maintain packet loss within threshold under variable network conditions.

assumptions: Customer will define acceptable network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth, carrier type) and packet loss threshold (≤1% or customer-defined) before detailed design.
Vehicle module can implement Tasking reception and Telemetry transmission with QoS tagging and prioritization.
Safety Services module can receive SafetyAlert and implement appropriate fallback actions (operator notification, speed reduction, manual operation mode).
Operator reaction time window is ≥200ms; FEC/retransmission latency must stay within this bound.
Network Link (underlying transport) is provided by infrastructure and is outside the scope of Teleoperation module design.
Teleoperation module can be implemented with distributed architecture (console-side and vehicle-side components) while maintaining logical cohesion.
open_risks: RISK: Network conditions may be worse than 'agreed' baseline, causing packet loss to exceed threshold despite DP-E implementation. Mitigation: SafetyAlert escalation and fallback to manual operation; customer must validate network SLA.
RISK: FEC overhead and retransmission latency may degrade real-time responsiveness if not carefully tuned. Mitigation: bounded-latency design and operator training.
RISK: Adaptive prioritization may deprioritize non-critical telemetry, reducing operator situational awareness. Mitigation: clear operator interface showing which telemetry is degraded.

RISK: Teleoperation module may become large and diffuse if not properly decomposed into sub-modules. Mitigation: decompose into Communication Controller, QoS Manager, FEC Engine, and Operator Interface in next level.

RISK: Integration with Vehicle and Safety Services modules may reveal interface mismatches or missing functionality. Mitigation: define clear interface contracts and conduct integration testing early.
evidence_needed: Customer-defined network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth, carrier type) and packet loss threshold.
Vehicle module interface specification for Tasking reception and Telemetry transmission (packet format, latency bounds, error handling).
Safety Services module interface specification for SafetyAlert reception and fallback action implementation.
Operator reaction time data and acceptable latency bounds for FEC/retransmission.
Network Link (transport) specification and quality guarantees (SLA).
Lab test results validating DP-E under simulated network conditions.
Field test results validating DP-E under real network conditions agreed with customer.
Integration test results validating Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Safety Services modules working together correctly.
